april.name: Spinach
april.description: A cool-weather powerhouse that thrives in early spring. Spinach grows quickly and tolerates light frost, making April ideal for cold zones.
april.howToPlant: Sow seeds half an inch deep directly in the garden, 2 inches apart in rows 12 inches apart. Thin seedlings to 4 to 6 inches as they grow.
april.careGuide: Keep soil consistently moist. Apply a nitrogen-rich fertilizer at planting. Use shade cloth to extend the harvest window as temperatures rise.
april.harvestTip: Begin harvesting outer leaves when plants reach 6 inches. Cut or pinch individual leaves rather than pulling the whole plant to keep it producing.

april.name: Lettuce
april.description: Loose-leaf lettuce varieties mature in 45 to 60 days and prefer the cool, mild temperatures of a cold-zone spring before summer heat triggers bolting.
april.howToPlant: Scatter seeds thinly on the soil surface and press in gently — lettuce needs light to germinate. Space rows 8 inches apart.
april.careGuide: Water lightly but frequently to keep shallow roots moist. Avoid overhead watering in the evening to prevent fungal issues.
april.harvestTip: Harvest outer leaves continuously for a cut-and-come-again yield. Pull the whole head before it bolts as summer heat arrives.

april.name: Chives
april.description: One of the first herbs to emerge in spring, chives are cold-hardy, low-maintenance, and productive for years once established.
april.howToPlant: Plant divisions or start from seed a quarter inch deep, 6 inches apart. Chives prefer a sunny to partly shaded spot with well-drained soil.
april.careGuide: Cut back to 2 inches after the first flush to encourage dense regrowth. Divide clumps every 3 years to maintain vigor.
april.harvestTip: Snip leaves with scissors 2 inches above the base. Harvest flowers as they open — they are edible and mildly onion-flavored.

april.name: Pansy
april.description: Pansies are cool-season stars — their bold faces and frost tolerance make them the go-to cold-zone flower for early spring color.
april.howToPlant: Plant transplants 6 to 8 inches apart in full sun or partial shade. Amend soil with compost before planting for best results.
april.careGuide: Deadhead spent blooms regularly to extend flowering. Feed with a balanced liquid fertilizer every two weeks.
april.harvestTip: Pansy flowers are edible and make elegant garnishes. Harvest in the morning when the blooms are freshly opened.

april.name: Tomato
april.description: The quintessential warm-season vegetable. April is the perfect time to transplant starts in moderate zones after the last frost date has passed.
april.howToPlant: Set transplants deep — bury two-thirds of the stem to encourage a strong root system. Space 24 to 36 inches apart in full sun and install a cage or stake at planting time.
april.careGuide: Water deeply 1 to 2 times per week at the base. Switch to a low-nitrogen, high-phosphorus fertilizer once flowering begins.
april.harvestTip: Harvest when the fruit is fully colored and slightly soft to the touch. Tomatoes left to ripen fully on the vine have the best flavor.

april.name: Pepper
april.description: Sweet and hot peppers thrive in the warming days of a moderate-zone April. They need heat to fruit, so starting with healthy transplants gives them a head start.
april.howToPlant: Plant transplants 18 inches apart in full sun. Peppers prefer slightly acidic, well-draining soil with plenty of organic matter.
april.careGuide: Keep soil evenly moist — inconsistent watering causes blossom drop. Mulch heavily to retain heat and moisture at the root zone.
april.harvestTip: Pick sweet peppers green for crunch or wait for full color for maximum sweetness. For hot varieties, a later harvest means more heat.

april.name: Basil
april.description: Basil loves the warm days of a moderate-zone spring. Plant it near your tomatoes for a classic pairing both in the garden and on the plate.
april.howToPlant: Sow seeds a quarter inch deep after soil temps hit 60 degrees, or set out transplants 12 inches apart in full sun with rich, moist soil.
april.careGuide: Pinch flower buds as they form to keep the plant producing flavorful leaves. Always water at the base to avoid leaf scorch.
april.harvestTip: Harvest leaves from the top down, cutting just above a leaf node each time to encourage bushy branching.

april.name: Cilantro
april.description: Cilantro prefers the mild temperatures of spring before summer heat triggers bolting. Succession sow every 3 weeks for a continuous supply.
april.howToPlant: Direct sow a quarter inch deep, 6 inches apart, in full sun to partial shade. Cilantro dislikes transplanting — always direct sow.
april.careGuide: Keep soil moist during germination. Once established, cilantro is relatively drought-tolerant. Thin to 6 inches apart.
april.harvestTip: Harvest outer leaves once plants reach 6 inches. Once flowers appear, harvest and dry the seeds (coriander) after they turn brown.

april.name: Zinnia
april.description: Zinnias are heat-tolerant, prolific bloomers that attract pollinators through summer. Start seeds now in moderate zones for bold summer color.
april.howToPlant: Direct sow seeds a quarter inch deep after frost risk passes, 6 inches apart in rows. Thin to 12 inches once seedlings establish.
april.careGuide: Water at the base to prevent powdery mildew. Feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer. Zinnias thrive in full sun with at least 6 hours a day.
april.harvestTip: Cut stems in the morning for the longest vase life. The more you cut, the more the plant produces — harvest freely.

april.name: Sweet Potato
april.description: Warm-zone gardens are ideal for sweet potatoes. April plantings give slips the long, hot season they need to develop full, sweet tubers.
april.howToPlant: Plant slips (rooted cuttings) 12 to 18 inches apart in raised rows 6 inches high. Full sun and sandy, well-draining soil are essential.
april.careGuide: Water deeply but infrequently once established. Avoid nitrogen-heavy fertilizers — they promote lush foliage at the expense of tubers.
april.harvestTip: Harvest 100 to 120 days after planting. Cure freshly dug tubers at 85 degrees with high humidity for 10 days to convert starches to sugar.

april.name: Eggplant
april.description: Eggplant thrives in the heat of warm zones. April transplants will be producing abundantly by midsummer if given consistent warmth and moisture.
april.howToPlant: Set transplants 18 to 24 inches apart in full sun. Eggplant is cold-sensitive — wait until nights consistently stay above 55 degrees.
april.careGuide: Use a balanced fertilizer at planting, switching to low-nitrogen once flowering begins. Consistent moisture is key to tender fruit.
april.harvestTip: Harvest when the skin is glossy and firm. Dull skin indicates the fruit is overripe. Always use pruning shears — do not twist or pull.

april.name: Lemongrass
april.description: Lemongrass is a warm-climate perennial that thrives in the heat. Plant it in spring and it will grow into a dramatic, fragrant clump by fall.
april.howToPlant: Divide and replant established clumps, or start from pots. Space 2 to 3 feet apart in full sun with rich, moist soil.
april.careGuide: Water regularly during the growing season. Apply a nitrogen-rich fertilizer monthly. Cut back to 6 inches in late fall.
april.harvestTip: Harvest the lower stalk by cutting at soil level or pulling outer stalks free. Use the tender lower portion after removing the tough outer layers.

april.name: Marigold
april.description: Marigolds are warm-zone workhorses — they naturally deter nematodes and aphids, attract pollinators, and bloom continuously through the heat.
april.howToPlant: Direct sow a quarter inch deep in full sun, or transplant starts 8 to 10 inches apart after the last frost date has passed.
april.careGuide: Deadhead regularly to encourage continuous bloom. Marigolds are drought-tolerant once established but bloom best with regular watering.
april.harvestTip: Harvest flower heads in full bloom for culinary use or dried arrangements. Petals are edible with a citrusy, slightly peppery flavor.

april.name: Okra
april.description: Okra loves tropical heat and humidity. In tropical zones, April planting sets up a long, abundant harvest that continues through summer.
april.howToPlant: Sow seeds 1 inch deep, 12 inches apart in full sun after soil reaches 65 degrees. Soak seeds overnight before planting to improve germination rates.
april.careGuide: Fertilize with a balanced NPK at planting and again when plants reach 12 inches tall. Water consistently — drought causes pods to become tough.
april.harvestTip: Harvest pods when 2 to 4 inches long. Pods become tough and fibrous quickly if left on the plant. Check daily during peak season.

april.name: Bitter Melon
april.description: A staple of tropical cuisine, bitter melon is a vigorous vining crop that thrives in year-round warm conditions with high humidity.
april.howToPlant: Direct sow or transplant starts after all cold risk passes. Provide a sturdy trellis — vines reach 10 to 12 feet over the season.
april.careGuide: Train vines along the trellis weekly. Feed biweekly with a balanced fertilizer. Keep soil consistently moist but never waterlogged.
april.harvestTip: Harvest while the skin is still green and firm, before the fruit yellows. Younger fruits are less bitter and more versatile for cooking.

april.name: Thai Basil
april.description: Thai basil is more heat-tolerant than Italian varieties and thrives in tropical conditions. Its anise-like flavor is essential in Southeast Asian cooking.
april.howToPlant: Transplant starts 12 inches apart in full sun after soil temps stabilize above 65 degrees. Thai basil is more drought-tolerant than sweet basil.
april.careGuide: Pinch flower heads as they appear to extend the leaf production period. The more you harvest, the bushier and more productive the plant becomes.
april.harvestTip: Harvest leaves and stems from the top of the plant. Flowers are also edible and make a beautiful, flavorful garnish.

april.name: Hibiscus
april.description: Hibiscus is a tropical classic that thrives in year-round warmth. Its large, dramatic flowers are also edible and widely used to make teas and beverages.
april.howToPlant: Plant in full sun, 3 to 5 feet apart. Hibiscus tolerates a range of soils but prefers well-draining, slightly acidic conditions.
april.careGuide: Water deeply 2 to 3 times per week in dry periods. Apply a high-potassium fertilizer during the blooming season to encourage continuous flowers.
april.harvestTip: Harvest flowers in full bloom in the morning. The calyx remaining after petals drop is the part used to make hibiscus tea.
